text.title: Multi-Project Hosting on One VPS Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every application of `Multi-Project Hosting on One VPS` must enforce. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Port allocation MUST live in `/srv/port-registry.txt` (or equivalent) committed via dotfiles. Implicit allocation = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Without a registry, two projects pick the same port; first one to start wins, second silently fails.

rule.statement: Each project MUST own a 100-port range (e.g. project-a: 8000-8099). Single ports = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Single-port allocation runs out as the project grows (api + worker + admin + websocket); range pre-allocates room.

rule.statement: Every project MUST live under `/srv/<project>/` with its own user (`<project>` or `srv-<project>`). Shared /srv = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Shared dirs mean one project's deploy can clobber another; per-user ownership enforces the boundary.

rule.statement: Only nginx (port 80/443) MUST be reachable from the internet. All app ports MUST bind to 127.0.0.1. Wildcard binds = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Direct exposure of app ports invites bypass of WAF + rate-limit; nginx is the single audit point.

rule.statement: Each project MUST get its own nginx vhost file under `/etc/nginx/sites-enabled/<project>.conf`. Combined files = REJECT.
rule.rationale: One vhost-per-file isolates blast radius of nginx -t failures; touching project B can't break project A's vhost parse.

rule.statement: When preconditions in `Applies If` fail OR any item in `Skip If` triggers, do not run this methodology. Document the skip rationale in the artefact's parent log.
rule.rationale: Misapplied methodologies are worse than no methodology — they consume cycles and produce false confidence.
